title: From Detection to Depletion
subtitle: Sustainability Constraints in Counter-Drone Defense
title_normalized: from detection to depletion: sustainability constraints in counter-drone defense

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief synthesizes the findings of EPINOVA-2025-01-RR into a policy-facing assessment of counter-drone defense sustainability. It argues that air defense in the Russia-Ukraine war is not failing primarily because drones cannot be intercepted, but because defense architectures struggle to remain economically and operationally sustainable under continuous low-cost saturation. The brief reframes counter-UAS evaluation from shoot-down ratios toward mission preservation, cost per loss avoided, and endurance under stress.
abstract: In the Russia-Ukraine war, air defense is not failing because drones cannot be intercepted, but because defenses struggle to remain economically and operationally sustainable under continuous saturation. Metrics focused on shoot-down rates systematically overstate effectiveness and underestimate structural exhaustion. Drawing on a Minimum Viable, Auditable (MVA) framework and observations from 2023-2025, this policy brief argues that cost per loss avoided, composite cost-loss indicators, and key asset preservation should guide counter-drone defense planning. It concludes that future air-defense effectiveness will be defined by sustainability and mission preservation rather than interception counts alone.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on AI-Enabled Warfare, Sustainability, and Global Security Governance
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on AI-enabled warfare, defense sustainability, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.framework_name: Minimum Viable, Auditable (MVA) Framework for Counter-Drone Defense Sustainability
methodology.case_context: Russia-Ukraine war, 2023-2025 counter-drone and air-defense sustainability patterns
methodology.core_problem: High intercept rates can mask structural exhaustion when defenders rely on costly interceptors against low-cost, high-frequency drone saturation.
central_claims: Air defense sustainability should be evaluated by mission preservation over time, not by shoot-down rates alone.
CPLA and composite cost-loss indicators may deteriorate before intercept rates decline, providing early warning of structural stress.
Force-structure choices determine whether counter-drone defense remains economically and operationally sustainable under saturation.
Low-cost terminal layers, when properly cued, flatten cost curves and preserve mission outcomes more robustly than heavy reliance on expensive surface-to-air missiles.

key_metrics.name: Cost per Loss Avoided
key_metrics.description: Measures how much it costs to preserve one unit of critical function.

key_metrics.name: Composite Cost-Loss Indicator
key_metrics.description: Detects whether cost pressure and residual loss are worsening together.

key_metrics.name: Key Asset Preservation Score
key_metrics.description: Measures whether core national functions are actually being preserved.

methodology.decision_rule: Minimize CPLA subject to maintaining KAPS above a defined mission threshold across plausible stress scenarios.

dimensions.name: Physical Detection and Engagement Limits
dimensions.description: Low-altitude clutter, weak radar and infrared signatures, and reduced observability constrain counter-drone detection and engagement.

dimensions.name: Temporal Decision Limits
dimensions.description: Sensor refresh, data fusion, authorization, and interceptor timelines may lag behind drone maneuver and attack cycles.

dimensions.name: Resource Sustainability Limits
dimensions.description: Magazine depth, replenishment speed, and fiscal burden become decisive under continuous saturation.

dimensions.name: Technology and Doctrine Mismatch
dimensions.description: Defensive systems optimized for fast, high-signature platforms face slow, low-signature, mass-produced threats.

dimensions.name: Operational Procedure Lag
dimensions.description: Legacy engagement rules, target classification standards, and human-centered authorization chains remain calibrated for manned or high-value threats rather than expendable drones.

dimensions.name: Mission Preservation
dimensions.description: Defense success is measured by preservation of critical national functions rather than total interception.

key_findings: In the Russia-Ukraine war, air defense is not failing because drones cannot be intercepted, but because defenses struggle to remain sustainable under continuous saturation.
Shoot-down ratios can systematically overstate effectiveness and underestimate structural exhaustion.
CPLA and composite cost-loss indicators deteriorate before intercept rates decline, making them useful early-warning indicators of structural stress.
Under saturation, force-structure choices dominate outcomes; reliance on expensive surface-to-air missiles sharply increases marginal defense costs.
Low-cost terminal layers such as guns, electronic warfare, and interceptor drones can flatten cost curves when properly cued and authorized.
High intercept rates can coexist with rapid magazine depletion, rising marginal defense costs, dependence on scarce interceptors, and readiness erosion.
Counter-UAS should be treated as an endurance capability involving stockpiles, production throughput, maintenance cycles, and surge capacity.
Future air-defense effectiveness will be defined by sustainability and mission preservation, not interception counts.

policy_implications.title: Adopt mission-based defense (KAPS-first)
policy_implications.description: Accept incomplete interception outside priority zones and concentrate protection, hardening, and rapid recovery on power, fuel, and other critical assets.

policy_implications.title: Rebalance toward low-cost terminal layers
policy_implications.description: Shift engagements against slow, low-signature drones away from high-end interceptors, supported by revised engagement rules and faster authorization pathways.

policy_implications.title: Invest in detection and track continuity
policy_implications.description: Improve engageability through better detection, cueing, track continuity, engagement authorities, and decision guidance.

policy_implications.title: Treat Counter-UAS as an endurance capability
policy_implications.description: Plan for stockpiles, production throughput, maintenance cycles, and surge capacity rather than peak performance alone.
